item.title: The weak shouldn't have to fear the powerful, and the powerful shouldn't simply be allowed to take whatever they want without consequence. Power should be a burden, not a shield.
item.description: "The Devil and the Dark Water" by Stuart Turton

item.title: For those of you who read the trigger warnings and said, "Accidental cannibalism?! Count me in!" This one's for you.
My one fear is that tomorrow I may die without having come to know myself.
They had only ever discussed books but what, in this life, is more personal than books?
item.description: "The Storied Life of A.J. Fikry" by Gabrielle Zevin

item.title: Do not ask for whom the bell tolls. Ask who would mourn in hearing it. If answer comes there none then more power to you (especially if you are planning an electrocution).
An animal may be ferocious and cunning enough, but it takes a real man to tell a lie.
item.description: "The Island of Dr. Moreau" by H.G. Wells

item.title: To everyone with religious trauma. I hope this helps heal you in some small, very weird way. Also to Andrew Scott. This is all your fault.
item.description: "Pounded by Produce: A Veggie Love Tale" by G.M. Fairy

item.title: I don't believe there has ever been a more terrible weapon in this world than the word they.
item.description: "Sky Full of Elephants" by Cebo Campbell

item.title: Amanda, You look at life through the eyes of a child, and I don't know where you got them, and it's making me very uncomfortable. Please stop.
item.description: "Witchcraft for Wayward Girls" by Grady Hendrix

item.title: You should try not to talk so much, friend. You'll sound far less stupid that way.
item.description: "Mistborn: The Final Empire" by Brando Sando

item.title: I must confess that I lost faith in the sanity of the world.
item.description: "The Island of Dr. Moreau" by H.G. Wells

item.title: It's good for a person to be terrorized by a goat. Hard to get high and mighty when there's something chasing you for vegetables
Ugh! How many stories about love, copulation, marriage and death already exist, not one of which tells the truth! How sick I am of well-constructed plots and brilliant writing!
It sounds plausible enough tonight, but wait until tomorrow. Wait for the common sense of the morning.
Finite does not mean much if you can't tell any practical difference between it and infinite.
item.description: "A Short Stay in Hell" by Steven L. Peck

item.title: If we couldn't feel pain, we wouldn't live long. We'd walk into fires because they look interesting and we'd have scratches that we don't notice and that go bad and we still don't notice; pain is useful.

The other kind is unhappiness. Not - passing discontent, I mean real unhappiness, the kind that lasts. And... that's for the same thing. It's there because you need to stop doing the thing that's making you unhappy, or it will kill you.
You know you can't make sweeping statements just on the strength of being exotic and foreign.

But I was too tired and too cold to argue, and for that moment at least I loved him for having made the idea of the funeral that bit less heavy, like he had put his shoulder under mine to share the weight.

I know what you mean. It was a bit good, is what you mean. That isn't evil. All it means is that you won't be one of those people who spiral off into guilty nightmares and never recover. Just... recognise the feeling and see the shape of it, and you won't aim it at the wrong person.

'There you are. I'm cooking, not very well; I was hoping you'd come round in time to properly supervise. Or at least tell me how in God's name you're supposed to cook quinoa.' 'Not like that. What's the point of you?' 'I'm ornamental.'
You see, we'd been taught that the devil was the worst thing in the world, but we were too young to understand that there were worse things than the devil.
item.description: "Witchcraft for Wayward Girls" by Grady Hendrix

item.title: It isn't too smart for a girl to be smart, her mom had always said.
item.description: "Witchcraft for Wayward Girls" by Grady Hendrix

item.title: Reading the wrong book is almost worse than not reading any book at all.
item.description: "Witchcraft for Wayward Girls" by Grady Hendrix

item.title: Our parents say the War is good. The president says the bomb is good. They tell us factories that poison the earth are good. They say drugs are bad, and burning draft cards is bad, and hippies are bad, and everyone who wants to stop the War and save the planet is bad. It's brainwashing! Everything they say is bad is good, and everything they say is good is bad. So maybe we should stop marching along like a bunch of sheep and start thinking for ourselves.
item.description: "Witchcraft for Wayward Girls" by Grady Hendrix

item.title: Kind of cool too. Like we didn't give a fuck about school nights.

As long as I kept reading I didn't have to think about this too much. When you're reading, the words of the book borrow the voice in your head. Words need a voice. The voice they use when you read is your voice. It's the voice your thoughts talk in. So if you give the voice to the book, your thoughts have no voice. They have to wait for the ends of paragraphs. They have to hold their breath until the chapter breaks.
'Or,' I said, 'I could be the same as other people, and just panic at things other people don't.'

'Like what?' she asked. 'What's there to panic about? Right here, I mean?' 'The whole thing, I guess.' 'The whole thing,' she said, making a gesture that included the world. 'The whole fucking thing.'
She said how everything happens for a reason. People will make a church out of any bad thing.
'I cannot take your burdens,' he went on. 'But if you let me, I can bear them with you for a while.'
item.description: "When the Tides Held the Moon" by Venessa Vida Kelley

item.title: 'So... you can't go anywhere unless someone carries you, right' the Thug asked. Cett grunted. 'Well then,' Ham said, sounding very pleased, 'I've got a good number of philosophical puzzles you might enjoy.'

Imagine if every single woman on the planet wanted something in exchange when she gave up her body. You'd all be ruling the place. An armed populace. Only men like me would stand a chance against you. And that's the last thing those assholes want, a world run by people like you and me.
item.description: "The Seven Husbands of Evelyn Hugo" by Taylor Jenkins Reid

item.title: 'So am I a whore or not?' 'Who knows?' he said. 'We're all whores, really, in some way or another.'
item.description: "The Seven Husbands of Evelyn Hugo" by Taylor Jenkins Reid

item.title: You do not know how fast you have been running, how hard you have been working, how truly exhausted you are, until someone stands behind you and says 'It's OK, you can fall down now. I'll catch you.' So I fell down. And Harry caught me.
item.description: "The Seven Husbands of Evelyn Hugo" by Taylor Jenkins Reid

item.title: Do not let what you gained this day be so easily lost.
item.description: "The Song of Achilles" by Madeline Miller

item.title: 'There is no law that the gods must be fair, Achilles,' Chiron said. 'And perhaps, it is the greater grief, after all, to be left on earth when another is gone.'
item.description: "The Song of Achilles" by Madeline Miller

item.title: Mr. Arens: This is the land of the free, sir! You would do well to show some respect! Dr. Gantz: Is it? Are you sure? Do you not read the news? Not only are American citizens in places like Little Rock and Greensboro organizing as we speak and demanding that their country grant them a modicum of the basic rights guaranteed in the Constitution, but this very committee has tied itself up in knots addressing threats to this nation that simply do not exist.
item.description: "When Women Were Dragons" by Kelly Barnhill

item.title: The beautiful thing about science is that we do not know what we cannot know and we will not know until we know. It requires an incredible amount of humility to be willing to be wrong nearly all the time. But we have to be willing to be wrong, and proven wrong, in order to increase knowledge overall. It is a thankless, and essential, job. Thank goodness.
item.description: "When Women Were Dragons" by Kelly Barnhill

item.title: 'I encourage you to consider a question: Who benefits, my dear, when you force yourself to not feel angry?' She tilted her head and looked at me so hard I thought she could see right into my bones. She raised her eyebrows. 'Clearly not you.'
item.description: "When Women Were Dragons" by Kelly Barnhill

item.title: People are awfully good at forgetting unpleasant things.
item.description: "When Women Were Dragons" by Kelly Barnhill

item.title: But I am a scientist, sirs, and my allegiance is not to this body, nor even to myself, but only to the truth. Who benefits when knowledge is buried? Who gains when science succumbs to political expediency?
item.description: "When Women Were Dragons" by Kelly Barnhill

item.title: Suffering doesn't make people good or noble. A little bit gives them perspective. A lot turns them cruel, and too much - you get a murder or a marvel, and neither of those are really people any more.

If you are sitting there saying, Oh, no, what a dreadful thing to tell children, then you can fuck off to Athens and enjoy their puke-inducing happily-ever-after stories where everyone prances joyfully off into the joyful sunset like demented morons. It is not a proper story unless everyone murders each other at the end.
When someone is that calm in a crisis, either they do not know what is happening, or you do not.
What you say will matter. Negotiate. Be Persephone. If you see Death coming, say, Fantastic, you are coming with me, and I am Queen.
Sometimes you just have to admit you are in a storm and stop trying to make any difference to where you are going. The wind is the wind, and Poseidon is mighty.
Do you know about friends? It is when you keep talking to another person for a good while but neither of you sets the other one on fire.

'Well, Poseidon is the sea, and the tide will not stop rising just because no one sacrifices to him. Time is there, death is there, the earth, war, love. Even if there is some terrible place three thousand years from now where nobody remembers any gods at all, there will still be the sea and love…' 'And madness. People will have just forgotten how to talk to them.'
Things had seemed a bit too much earlier, but sometimes what you need to remember is that everything is fine as long as you are not currently on fire.
I think many of his stories, though, come down to one fundamental idea: neglect catharsis, and you will go mad.
Humans are weird animals; partly we are wild, and partly we are clockwork. Unholy devices, all of us—mechanisms bolted onto bone.
You know the old saying: A cat a day keeps the doctor away. Cats are more effective than any other medicine out there.
item.description: "We'll Prescribe You A Cat" by Syou Ishida

item.title: The amount of time you spent together probably matters, but less time doesn't mean less love. Whether it's a day or a year, human or cat, and even if we may never see them again, there are those who are irreplaceable in our lives.
item.description: "We'll Prescribe You A Cat" by Syou Ishida

item.title: It must be nice to be a cat with not a care in the world.
item.description: "We'll Prescribe You A Cat" by Syou Ishida

item.title: There was something comforting about watching a cat going about on a little adventure.
item.description: "We'll Prescribe You A Cat" by Syou Ishida

item.title: It is frighteningly easy to get lost in your own subconscious; any place you think you know is different after dark.
The most arresting odes to slumber are spoken by those who cannot have it.
How inappropriate to call this planet Earth, when clearly it is Ocean.
The world was bigger, stranger, richer, and wilder than I had a right to ask for.

The ocean was forever unfolding, forever exploring, forever tinkering with form, and every part of it was busy talking about what was all around. So was she. So was every being that came from those waters. Which meant every living thing.
We make things that we hope will be bigger than us, and then we are desolate when that is what they become.
Sow the wind and reap the maelstrom.
Play was evolution's way of building brains, and any creature with a brain as developed as a giant oceanic manta sure used it. If you want to make something smarter, teach it to play.
Of all the things we humans excel at, moving the goalposts may be our best trick. The moment advanced AIs get good at that, they'll have passed the real Turing test.
Why can humans not use their millions of words to simply tell one another what they desire?
item.description: "Remarkably Bright Creatures" by Shelby Van Pelt

item.title: Tova has always felt more than a bit of empathy for the sharks, with their never-ending laps around the tank. She understands what it means to never be able to stop moving, lest you find yourself unable to breathe.
item.description: "Remarkably Bright Creatures" by Shelby Van Pelt

item.title: Secrets are everywhere. Some humans are crammed full of them. How do they not explode? It seems to be a hallmark of the human species: abysmal communication skills.
item.description: "Remarkably Bright Creatures" by Shelby Van Pelt

item.title: Smart cookie. I am smart, but I am not a snack object dispensed from a packaged food machine. What a preposterous thing to say.
item.description: "Remarkably Bright Creatures" by Shelby Van Pelt

item.title: Day 1,361 of My Captiv- Oh, Let Us Cut the Shit, Shall We? We Have a Ring to Retrieve.
item.description: "Remarkably Bright Creatures" by Shelby Van Pelt

item.title: Ah, to be a human, for whom bliss can be achieved by mere ignorance! Here, in the kingdom of animals, ignorance is dangerous. The poor herring dropped into the tank lacks any awareness of the shark lurking below. Ask the herring whether what he does not know can hurt him.
item.description: "Remarkably Bright Creatures" by Shelby Van Pelt
